stories.title: Principle Construction Completes Warehouse Expansion in Hampshire
stories.excerpt: Principle Construction Corp. has completed a complex warehouse renovation for POLI FILM America, Inc., located at One Elgiloy Drive in Hampshire, Illinois. The project provides the protective plastic film manufacturer with a streamlined, rectangular floor plan designed to optimize its high-volume manufacturing operations. The centerpiece of the project involved a creative structural solution that included relocating 12 precast concrete panels from an existing 133-foot wall. “Completing this project on schedule allows POLI FILM to scale their operations without the overhead of a total relocation,” says Mark Augustyn, Principle COO. “By literally moving the walls, we provided a cost-effective expansion that maximizes every square foot of their existing footprint.” The addition of 38,956 square feet to the manufacturing facility included a new entry door and a drive-in door, a gas-fired make-up air unit, a ballasted roof, and lighting. The project was managed by Principle’s Andrew Mackintosh, with architectural designs provided by Harris Architects. The post Principle Construction Completes Warehouse Expansion in Hampshire appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Principle Construction completed 38,956-sf warehouse expansion for POLI FILM in Hampshire, IL.
Project used 'moving walls' technique—relocated 12 precast panels—to avoid costly relocation.
Manufacturing optimization signals stable/growing demand for industrial space near Chicago.
stories.summary: Principle Construction completed a 38,956-square-foot warehouse expansion for plastic film manufacturer POLI FILM in Hampshire, Illinois, using a cost-effective structural solution that relocated precast concrete panels rather than requiring full relocation. The project preserved the company's operational continuity while maximizing existing footprint. This type of incremental expansion signals ongoing industrial demand and operational confidence in the Chicago-area market.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact to multifamily development. Industrial optimization and manufacturing confidence in the Chicago exurbs may correlate with job stability and wage growth in that corridor, supporting secondary demand for rental housing, but this is indirect. Story primarily signals industrial real estate activity, not housing supply, cost, or demand shifts.

stories.title: National Geographic Society Museum of Exploration in DC Debuts 100K-SF Space
stories.excerpt: JLL’s Project and Development Services (PDS) business announced its role in the expansion and redevelopment of the National Geographic Society’s Museum of Exploration. The newly reopened National Geographic Museum of Exploration on 17th Street NW features a $300 million expansion spanning over 100,000 square feet. The complex project included the ground-up construction of an expanded museum and nighttime courtyard experience, education center, retail space and the transformation of an existing auditorium into a state-of-the-art theater.“We are thrilled to debut the National Geographic Society’s Museum of Exploration,” said Mark Ahern, Managing Director, JLL, PDS Projects. “This project really showcases JLL’s expertise in handling large-scale, multi-faceted expansions while maintaining daily operations of an occupied headquarters.”Ahern and Hali Voycik, Vice President of PDS Projects, coordinated a multidisciplinary team of specialty design consultants, while the broader team, including Michael Mensing, Senior Project Manager; Kelly Ring, Senior Project Manager; and Nicole Roethel, Project Manager, oversaw contractor performance, regulatory compliance, risk management and construction activities. The post National Geographic Society Museum of Exploration in DC Debuts 100K-SF Space appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: National Geographic Museum of Exploration opened with $300M investment, 100,000+ sf in Washington DC.
Project included ground-up construction, new theater, retail, education center.
Major institutional capital deployment in DC real estate signals continued urban investment.
stories.summary: JLL's Project and Development Services oversaw the $300 million expansion and redevelopment of the National Geographic Society Museum of Exploration on 17th Street NW in Washington DC, delivering over 100,000 square feet of new space including ground-up construction, a state-of-the-art theater, retail, and education facilities. The project maintained daily HQ operations during construction. Major institutional capital deployment in core DC real estate underscores continued urban investment confidence.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily development. Large institutional projects in core urban markets (DC office-to-mixed-use conversions) may improve streetscape economics and foot traffic, supporting adjacent multifamily rents, but this is a companion effect rather than a driver of housing supply or demand. No signal on residential construction or market fundamentals.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos secured $28M construction financing (70% LTV) for 111-unit mixed-use project in Friendswood, TX.
Friendswood suburb (25 mi SE of Houston, 42k residents) showing significant pipeline: $750M and $1B projects underway.
Mid-construction financing indicates lender confidence; completion slated 2028.
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28 million in construction financing from S3 Capital (70% LTV) for The Albritton, a 111-unit mixed-use project in Friendswood, Texas, 25 miles southeast of Houston. The deal carries the project through completion, expected in 2028. Tannos is also developing two major projects: Friendswood City Center ($750M mixed-use, started spring 2024) and Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres with retail and hotel). The financing success signals lender appetite for Houston-area suburban residential.
stories.impact: Houston-area suburban market (Friendswood/Magnolia submarket) showing strong supply pipeline and capital availability. Tannos's three concurrent projects ($1.828B+ in gross development value) suggest confidence in downstream rental demand in outer Houston rings. Multifamily developers should watch Friendswood absorption and rent trajectories post-2027 completion; early phase leasing will signal whether outer Houston offers yield opportunity ahead of supply saturation.

stories.title: NAI Hiffman, GREA Close Sale of Four-Building Portfolio in Hinsdale
stories.excerpt: NAI Hiffman and GREA announced the closing of the Downtown Hinsdale Portfolio, a four-parcel, multi-tenant commercial property located at 10 W. Chicago Avenue in downtown Hinsdale, Illinois. NAI Hiffman Senior Vice President Arthur Burrows, along with GREA Senior Managing Directors William Montana and Christopher Sackley and Associate Kyle Jamison, represented ownership as the sole and exclusive agents in the transaction. The 37,540-square-foot site is improved with a single-story barrel-truss-roof building and a partial two- and single-story building totaling approximately 19,690 net rentable square feet, along with a 49-space surface parking lot. The barrel-truss building at 10 W. Chicago was originally constructed in 1926 for the Weiss Auto Shop, with 26-28 W. Chicago added circa 1932. The sellers acquired the property from the Comiskey family, original owners of the Chicago White Sox, in 1964. The income-producing asset features five ground-level retail spaces and eight second-floor offices. The post NAI Hiffman, GREA Close Sale of Four-Building Portfolio in Hinsdale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NAI Hiffman/GREA closed sale of four-parcel downtown Hinsdale portfolio, 19,690-sf NRA.
Historic building (1926 Weiss Auto Shop); five retail, eight office spaces; 49-space parking.
Heritage Chicago-area commercial asset sale; no detail on buyer demand or price trend.
stories.summary: NAI Hiffman and GREA closed the sale of the Downtown Hinsdale Portfolio, a four-parcel, 37,540-square-foot site with 19,690 net rentable square feet at 10 West Chicago Avenue. The property includes a 1926 barrel-truss building (originally Weiss Auto Shop), five ground-level retail spaces, eight second-floor offices, and 49 parking spaces. The site has heritage lineage to the Comiskey family (Chicago White Sox owners).
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. Historic downtown Hinsdale commercial portfolio sale reflects investor sentiment on suburban Chicago retail-office mixed-use, but provides no signal on residential demand, supply, or capital availability. No indication of conversion potential or multifamily zoning interest.

stories.title: Colliers Brokers Sale of 76K-SF Aerospace Manufacturing Campus in MD
stories.excerpt: Colliers has facilitated the successful sale of 4501 Boston Way and 4701 Forbes Boulevard, two highly specialized manufacturing and engineering facilities totaling approximately 76,000 square feet in Lanham, Maryland. The acquisition reunites Genesis Engineering Solutions’ headquarters and manufacturing facilities with its operating business. Colliers represented AR2 Southgate, LLC, an entity owned by Genesis Engineering Solutions founder Robert Rashford, in the disposition of the real estate. The assignment followed the October 2025 sale of the Genesis operating business. Colliers Vice Presidents Dan Gaffey and Chandler Pace served as the exclusive advisors to the seller. The properties will continue to serve as Genesis Engineering Solutions’ longtime headquarters and manufacturing facilities. “The sale of 4501 Boston Way and 4701 Forbes Boulevard highlights the continued demand for specialized manufacturing facilities in the Washington, D.C. region,” said Gaffey. “Given the property’s longstanding role in Genesis Engineering Solutions’ operations, the transaction provided a natural fit between the facilities and a buyer closely connected to the company’s future.” Connect Industrial West | August 20 | Irvine, CAOn August 20, Connect Industrial West will bring together the owners, investors, developers, brokers, lenders, and occupiers driving industrial real estate across the Western U.S. Join top decision-makers for an afternoon of market insights, dealmaking opportunities, and networking with the industry’s most influential players. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Colliers Brokers Sale of 76K-SF Aerospace Manufacturing Campus in MD appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Colliers brokered sale of 76,000-sf aerospace manufacturing campus (Genesis Engineering) in Lanham, MD.
Transaction reunited Genesis HQ and manufacturing after October 2025 business sale.
Colliers cited 'continued demand for specialized manufacturing' in Washington DC region.
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the sale of two specialized manufacturing and engineering facilities totaling 76,000 square feet in Lanham, Maryland (4501 Boston Way and 4701 Forbes Boulevard), bringing Genesis Engineering Solutions' headquarters back under unified ownership following the October 2025 sale of its operating business. The transaction underscores continued demand for specialized manufacturing facilities in the Washington DC region.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Industrial real estate demand near DC signals economic resilience and aerospace/defense sector stability in the region, which supports job and wage growth. Lanham (Prince George's County, MD suburbs) may see secondary rental demand from aerospace/engineering employment, but this story does not signal residential supply, entitlements, financing, or cost shifts relevant to multifamily development strategy.

stories.title: Buckling scare puts MetroLoft’s ‘scoop-and-stack’ playbook under microscope
stories.excerpt: The dramatic structural failure at MetroLoft’s conversion of Pfizer’s former Midtown headquarters is drawing attention to one of the developer’s signature redevelopment techniques. The temporary evacuation around 235 East 42nd Street earlier this month, after columns buckled and floors sagged during construction, has brought renewed focus to MetroLoft founder Nathan Berman’s “scoop-and-stack” approach, according to Crain’s. While investigators continue probing what caused the incident, engineers and developers say the method itself underscores the growing complexity of converting postwar office towers into apartments. The strategy essentially hollows out portions of a building’s lower floors, creating interior lightwells while transferring that unused […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MetroLoft structural failure at Pfizer HQ conversion (235 E 42nd St, NYC) during 'scoop-and-stack' redevelopment.
Technique: hollow lower floors, add lightwells, transfer density to cantilevered rooftop expansion (973k → 1.3M sf).
Column buckling/floor sagging halts work; engineers scrutinizing feasibility and safety of postwar office conversions.
stories.summary: Columns buckled and floors sagged at MetroLoft's conversion of Pfizer's former Midtown Manhattan headquarters (235 East 42nd Street), temporarily evacuating the site. The failure spotlights MetroLoft's signature 'scoop-and-stack' technique: carving out lower-floor space to create interior lightwells and transferring that lost development potential to new floors (including 15-story cantilevers) at the tower top. The project aimed to expand from 973,000 to 1.3 million square feet. Investigators continue probing the cause, but engineers and developers are now reassessing the method's complexity and structural feasibility.
stories.impact: CRITICAL for NYC office-to-residential conversion market. Structural failure and engineering scrutiny will delay and increase hard costs on MetroLoft's Pfizer conversion and other similar 'scoop-and-stack' projects in Midtown Manhattan. May trigger design-code review, extended permitting, and insurance/lender hesitation on cantilevered or heavily-modified postwar towers. Developers pursuing office conversions in NYC should assume longer timelines, higher contingencies, and tighter lender underwriting on structural modifications. Conversely, demand for residential units in Midtown remains strong; pricing/unit economics may improve if conversion supply is constrained.

stories.title: Phoenix Hopes to Kickstart Development Along Salt River
stories.excerpt: Looking down from above, the land running along the Salt River is a hodgepodge of service yards, sand-and-gravel operations, and vacant desert that often serves as a dumping ground. The city of Phoenix wants to change that. The Phoenix Business Journal reports that it’s firming up land-use policies and identifying key sites within a 4,000-acre parcel along the Salt River, where it’s aiming to attract new investment and development. The city only owns one of the sites – a 30-acre parcel at the southeast corner of Central Avenue and Park Drive, which is near a light rail station and a future pedestrian bridge. A mixed-use campus with businesses and a community building are envisioned there. A similar development is proposed at another targeted site off 7th Avenue. The other two sites are proposed to include a recreation center, while an “ecological hotel,” a research campus and multifamily housing are proposed at the other. The post Phoenix Hopes to Kickstart Development Along Salt River appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Phoenix city targeting 4,000-acre Salt River corridor for mixed-use development; owns one 30-acre parcel near light rail.
Proposed uses: mixed-use campus, recreation, ecology hotel, research, multifamily housing.
Low current utilization (service yards, sand, vacant desert) indicates large greenfield opportunity.
stories.summary: Phoenix is firming up land-use policies and targeting key sites within a 4,000-acre parcel along the Salt River for new mixed-use investment. The city owns one 30-acre site at Central Avenue and Park Drive, near a light rail station and future pedestrian bridge, planned for mixed-use with business and community building. Additional sites are proposed for recreation, ecological hotel, research campus, and multifamily housing. The corridor currently comprises service yards, sand-and-gravel operations, and vacant land.
stories.impact: MAJOR SIGNAL for Phoenix multifamily development. City-led 4,000-acre corridor master planning with transit connectivity (light rail) and public-sector land contribution suggests entitlement-friendly zoning environment and reduced land cost/availability friction. Developers should track Salt River master plan approval timeline and light rail opening; proximity to transit + density upzoning will drive multifamily demand. Early site acquisition near Central/Park Drive and parallel light rail stations could yield significant rent/unit economics advantage as infrastructure and zoning certainty crystalize.

stories.title: Brookfield Takes a Major Stake in Healthpeak Properties’ $2.1B Portfolio
stories.excerpt: Brookfield Asset Management has established a joint venture with Denver-based real estate investment trust (REIT) Healthpeak Properties for an interest in its vast medical property portfolio. The capital partnership, announced Monday, hands Brookfield and its affiliates a 49 percent interest in Healthpeak’s 86 outpatient medical buildings nationwide, valued at approximately $2.1 billion. The collection of facilities totals roughly 5.6 million square feet and spans 11 states, including Kentucky, Indiana, Pennsylvania, Arkansas, Illinois, Minnesota, New Jersey and New York. The portfolio is currently 95 percent leased, according to the announcement. Newmark acted as a financial adviser in the transaction, and Kirkland & Ellis served as a legal adviser to Brookfield. Healthpeak, formed in 1985, owns, operates and develops properties for the healthcare industry, including life sciences laboratories and senior housing communities. The REIT is involved in approximately 700 properties nationwide, according to its website. “Brookfield’s reputation, scale and long-term investment approach complement our deep sector expertise and leading operating platform,” Scott Brinker, president and CEO of Healthpeak, said in a statement. “This transaction advances our capital allocation priorities and highlights our unique ability to capture the favorable tailwinds driving demand for outpatient care.” Healthpeak will retain a 51 percent controlling interest in the portfolio, thereby preserving its ownership and operational control while accessing new, long-term capital. The REIT received gross proceeds of roughly $1 billion from the deal. Brookfield’s real estate business line, led by Ben Brown, has been hard at work over the last year and a half. It has executed about $32.3 billion in acquisitions and raised $13.2 billion in new capital. One of those acquisitions was just completed Monday, with Brookfield teaming with the Canada Pension Plan Investment
bullets: Brookfield Asset Management acquires 49% stake in Healthpeak Properties' 86 outpatient medical buildings ($2.1B valuation).
Portfolio: 5.6M sf across 11 states (KY, IN, PA, AR, IL, MN, NJ, NY), 95% leased.
Joint venture signals institutional capital consolidation in medical real estate; non-multifamily asset class.
stories.summary: Brookfield Asset Management established a joint venture with Denver-based Healthpeak Properties, acquiring a 49 percent interest in Healthpeak's portfolio of 86 outpatient medical buildings across 11 states, valued at approximately $2.1 billion. The portfolio totals roughly 5.6 million square feet and is currently 95 percent leased. Facilities span Kentucky, Indiana, Pennsylvania, Arkansas, Illinois, Minnesota, New Jersey, and New York.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. Brookfield's capital deployment into medical real estate reflects institutional investor appetite for healthcare assets but does not signal shifts in residential supply, demand, capital availability, or cost. May indicate capital rotation away from office/retail (declining sectors) toward healthcare and life sciences, but this is a companion asset-class observation, not a residential driver.

stories.title: Insurance, Real Estate Firms Take 57K SF at SL Green’s 1185 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: Two companies signed recent deals at SL Green Realty’s 1185 Avenue of the Americas. In the largest lease, international specialty insurance firm Ryan Specialty signed a 10-year renewal and expansion lease at the 42-story office property, taking 29,166 square feet across the building’s entire 38th floor, the landlord announced. The company will move within the building from part of the 23rd floor, where it occupied 12,803 square feet. The asking rent was not disclosed, but the average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from Colliers. Ryan Specialty was represented in the deal by Howard Grufferman, Catherine Soderquist and Ryan Barr from Colliers. The landlord was represented by Brian Waterman, John Fanuzzi, Brent Ozarowski, David Waterman and Kevin Sullivan from Newmark. Colliers did not immediately respond to a request for comment, while Newmark declined to comment. Meanwhile, property management company Solil Management signed a new five-and-a-half-year lease at the property, taking 27,508 square feet on the building’s entire 10th floor. The landlord and the tenant each represented themselves in the deal, SL Green noted. “We’re delighted to have both of these highly regarded firms as part of the building’s premier tenant roster,” Steven Durels, executive vice president and director of leasing and real property at SL Green, said in a statement. “These transactions underscore the ongoing strength of tenant demand for well-located, high-quality office space in Midtown Manhattan.” Other tenants at 1185 Avenue of the Americas include energy company Hess as well as law firm Groombridge, Wu, Baughman & Stone. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Ryan Specialty (insurance firm) renewed/expanded at SL Green's 1185 Ave of Americas: 29,166 sf (38th floor), 10-year lease.
Company moved from 23rd floor (12,803 sf); Midtown office asking rent $84.99/sf (Q2 2026).
Strong office leasing activity in Midtown Manhattan; SL Green reaching full occupancy.
stories.summary: Ryan Specialty, an international specialty insurance firm, signed a 10-year renewal and expansion lease at SL Green Realty's 1185 Avenue of the Americas, taking 29,166 square feet on the 38th floor. The company upgraded from 12,803 square feet on the 23rd floor. Additionally, property management company Solil Management signed a 5.5-year lease. Midtown Manhattan office asking rent averaged $84.99 per square foot in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily relevance. Office leasing strength in Midtown Manhattan (Ryan Specialty expansion, full occupancy at 1185 Ave) signals continued demand for Class A office space and tech/professional services employment stability. However, this reinforces office recovery rather than residential market fundamentals. Multifamily developers should note: strong office rents and leasing may compete for prime Manhattan real estate capital; however, housing remains supply-constrained, potentially supporting multifamily rent premiums independent of office trends.

stories.title: Witkoff, Monroe Capital Land $302M Construction Loan for Downtown Miami Rental
stories.excerpt: Witkoff and Monroe Capital have secured a $302.6 million loan construction loan to build a multifamily tower in Downtown Miami, property records show. The high-rise will include about 890 units and 15,000 square feet of ground-floor retail space at 700 North Miami Avenue, between the MiamiCentral station and the 27-acre Miami Worldcenter master development, per a source familiar with the plans. Apollo Global Management’s Athene Annuity and Life Company and J.P. Morgan Chase took a loan with $57.3 million in outstanding debt and increased it by $245.3 million, the source told Commercial Observer. The construction represents the first phase of a larger mixed-use project. In 2022, Witkoff and Monroe Capital filed plans to build 2,195 residential units, 540,000 square feet of office space and 50,000 square feet of retail space across three towers. The prior year, the joint venture had purchased the 4.7-acre, vacant site for $94 million. The site had housed the Miami Arena, where Miami’s NBA team, the Heat, played before it was demolished in 2008. The development marks the second project between New York-based developer Witkoff and Chicago-based Monroe Capital. Across Biscayne Bay in Miami Beach, the pair are building the oceanfront Shore Club luxury condo development, where a penthouse remains under contract for $120 million. Alex Witkoff, son of founder Steve Witkoff, runs the firm after the patriarch became President Donald Trump’s special diplomatic envoy in 2025. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Witkoff/Monroe Capital secured $302.6M construction loan for 890-unit multifamily tower, Downtown Miami.
700 North Miami Avenue: 15,000 sf retail; between MiamiCentral transit station and Miami Worldcenter.
Phase 1 of larger 2,195-unit, 540k-sf office, 50k-sf retail master plan; site purchased 2021 for $94M.
stories.summary: Witkoff and Monroe Capital secured a $302.6 million construction loan from Athene Annuity and Life Company and J.P. Morgan Chase to build an 890-unit multifamily tower with 15,000 square feet of ground-floor retail at 700 North Miami Avenue, positioned between MiamiCentral station and the 27-acre Miami Worldcenter master development. This is Phase 1 of a larger project that includes 2,195 total residential units, 540,000 square feet of office, and 50,000 square feet of retail. The development team purchased the 4.7-acre vacant site in 2021 for $94 million.
stories.impact: CRITICAL for Miami multifamily market. Large construction loan ($302.6M) in soft/moderate rate environment signals strong lender appetite for transit-proximate, mixed-use residential in Downtown Miami. 890-unit first phase of 2,195-unit master plan represents significant supply hitting market; absorption and rent trajectory will signal market saturation risk. Developers should monitor project leasing pace and achieved rents (especially in context of competing downtown Miami projects like OKO Lilli, 13th Floor Nobu tower—see article 16) to calibrate entry timing and pro-forma assumptions for similar downtown/Brickell submarket projects.

stories.title: Instacart Takes 26K SF at ESRT’s 111 West 33rd Street
stories.excerpt: Grocery delivery app Instacart is getting a bigger home in Midtown. The San Francisco-based technology company has signed a 26,134-square-foot lease at Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s 111 West 33rd Street, the landlord announced Monday. The seven-year lease was signed during the second quarter of 2026, according to ESRT, at an asking rent of $75 per square foot. In 2023, Instacart signed a sublease at 50 West 23rd Street for 21,000 square feet, taking space previously occupied by software company Bizzabo, Commercial Observer reported at the time. It is unclear if Instacart will exit 50 West 23rd Street permanently, or if the new lease at 111 West 33rd Street is an expansion of its current New York City footprint. Instacart did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Additionally, ESRT announced Monday that biopharmaceutical company Hansa Biopharma inked a 7,052-square-foot lease at 111 West 33rd Street, bringing the 26-story building to 100 percent leased. The firm also noted that its building at 1350 Broadway is 100 percent leased. “100 percent leased at both 111 West 33rd Street and 1350 Broadway is a testament to the strength of these assets and the demand for well-located, modernized and amenitized office space in New York City,” Ryan Kass, executive vice president, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT, said in a statement. Instacart was represented by Josh Pernice, Timothy Kazul and Conor Famulener from CBRE, while Hansa Biopharma was represented by Matthew Leon and Jake Leon from Newmark. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini and Kerry Lavelle from ESRT, along with Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Neil Rubin, Cole Gendels and Zachary Weil from Newmark, represented the property owner in both deals. The brokers did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Built in 1954, 111 West 33rd Street is home to tenants such as consulting firm Clearview Healthcare Partners and data firm the Penta Group. The property a block left
bullets: Instacart (grocery delivery app) leased 26,134 sf at ESRT's 111 W 33rd St, Midtown Manhattan, 7-year term.
Asking rent: $75/sf; building now 100% leased; Instacart's second Manhattan office (prior: 21k sf sublease in 2023).
Tech company expansion signals continued Midtown demand despite office sector headwinds.
stories.summary: Instacart, the San Francisco-based grocery delivery technology company, signed a 26,134-square-foot lease at Empire State Realty Trust's 111 West 33rd Street in Midtown Manhattan for seven years at $75 per square foot asking rent. The company previously occupied a 21,000-square-foot sublease at 50 West 23rd Street starting in 2023. The 111 West 33rd Street lease brings the 26-story building to 100 percent occupancy.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. Instacart expansion signals tech sector demand for Midtown office space, but does not affect residential supply, demand, capital, or cost. Office sector stability may reinforce employment and wage support in Manhattan, indirectly benefiting residential demand, but this is secondary to housing supply constraints and rents driven by zoning/conversion feasibility and capital availability.

stories.title: Brookfield, CPP Investments to Acquire LXP Industrial Trust for $5.2B
stories.excerpt: Brookfield Asset Management is lined up for a big industrial acquisition. The global alternative asset manager with more than $1 trillion of assets under management has teamed with the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) to acquire LXP Industrial Trust in an all-cash deal valued at approximately $5.2 billion, the firms announced Monday. LXP, a real estate investment trust (REIT) focused on Class A warehouse and distribution investments, owns one of the largest portfolios of warehouse and logistics facilities in the country, comprising approximately 53 million square feet across 108 properties in 12 markets in the Sun Belt and Midwest, according to the announcement. Thomas W. Eglin Jr., chairman and CEO of LXP, said in a statement that the transaction is the “culmination of the LXP team’s successful execution of [its] strategic plan to transform LXP into a pure-play industrial REIT.” Meanwhile, the deal was part of a continued interest in the U.S. industrial sector for CPP Investments, according to the firm’s managing director and head of real estate, Sophie van Oosterom. “The industrial sector, particularly in the U.S., continues to offer attractive long-term investment opportunities, supported by structural demand drivers, including domestic manufacturing, evolving global supply chains and population growth across key Sun Belt markets,” van Oosterom said in a statement. “We look forward to partnering with Brookfield and combining their operating expertise with a well-positioned portfolio to generate sustainable investment returns.” Lowell Baron, CEO of Brookfield Real Estate, added: “The acquisition aligns with our strategy of investing in high-quality real estate with durable cash flows and opportunities to create value through active asset management.” The merger is expected to close in the fourth quarter of 2026. Bank of America Securities and J.P. Morgan Securities acted as financial advisers in the deal. Under the terms of the agreement, LXP
bullets: Brookfield Asset Management and CPP Investments acquire LXP Industrial Trust for $5.2B (all-cash).
LXP portfolio: 53M sf across 108 properties in 12 Sun Belt/Midwest markets.
Mega-cap alternative asset manager consolidation of Class A warehouse/logistics assets; signals sustained capital confidence in industrial.
stories.summary: Brookfield Asset Management and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) announced an all-cash acquisition of LXP Industrial Trust for approximately $5.2 billion. LXP owns one of the largest Class A warehouse and logistics portfolios in the country, comprising approximately 53 million square feet across 108 properties in 12 markets in the Sun Belt and Midwest.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. Mega-cap acquisition of industrial REIT signals institutional capital confidence in logistics/warehouse sector across Sun Belt (major multifamily growth markets). Sustained industrial capital deployment may indicate broader economic growth confidence in those regions, supporting employment and migration—indirect drivers of multifamily demand. However, industrial and multifamily compete for capital at margin; heavy institutional consolidation in logistics may limit financing availability for residential development, particularly in secondary Sun Belt markets.

stories.title: Stetson University plans Tampa development as higher ed bets on mixed-use districts
stories.excerpt: Stetson University is joining the higher education trend of investing in mixed-use districts, with the redevelopment of its Tampa Law Center. Stetson is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies on plans to develop 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail space and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law center, on the 6.5-acre site in the Tampa Heights district, according to ConnectCRE.  Stetson University acquired the property from the City of Tampa in 2002, according to the Tampa Business Journal. It was previously the site of the Tampa Heights police station.  The university’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stetson University partnering with Bromley Companies to redevelop 6.5-acre Tampa Law Center.
Project: 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500-sf retail, 440k-sf office, new law center.
Higher education trend: monetize real estate via mixed-use development; mirrors USF Fletcher District ($268M, 27 acres).
stories.summary: Stetson University is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies to redevelop its 6.5-acre Tampa Law Center campus, planning 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail, 440,000 square feet of office, and a new law facility. The project requires Tampa City Council rezoning approval; construction is expected to start late 2026. This mirrors a national higher education trend exemplified by USF's $268 million Fletcher District on its Tampa campus. The development will be phased over time.
stories.impact: SIGNIFICANT for Tampa multifamily market and mixed-use development model. Institutional (university) real estate monetization drives major mixed-use supply in urban core (Tampa Heights district). 670-unit apartment component represents material supply addition; concurrent hotel and office may create amenity density. Developers should expect: (1) Stetson project will compete for local financing, construction labor, and renter pool; (2) success will attract other institutional asset holders to unlock real estate via mixed-use; (3) zoning approval signals city openness to density. Monitor Stetson phasing and early-phase leasing (late 2026+) to assess rent achievability and market absorption capacity in Tampa Heights/downtown corridor.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for $400M fraud on fake San Gabriel River mixed-use project.
Williamson County jury ordered $300M punitive damages for breach/fraud; defendants swindled contractor of $21M.
Pair previously hit with SEC restraining order/asset freeze in 2023 for alleged $130M Ponzi scheme (DFW Indian-American community).
stories.summary: A Williamson County, Texas jury found Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud related to a fake 400-acre mixed-use development on the San Gabriel River near Dripping Springs. The defendants were ordered to pay $400 million in damages (including $300 million in punitive damages) to Energy Commissioning Inc., a Georgetown-based company they hired to perform $21 million of infrastructure work for the non-existent project. This is the pair's second fraud allegation; the SEC hit them with a temporary restraining order and asset freeze in 2023 for an alleged $130 million Ponzi scheme targeting DFW's Indian-American community.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily development impact. High-profile fraud conviction in Central Texas real estate highlights lender/investor due diligence importance but does not signal market supply, demand, capital availability, or cost shifts. May reinforce lender caution on developer sponsors with thin track records or concentrated investor bases, but is an outlier enforcement action rather than a systemic market signal.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revised Marina Safeway redevelopment: 25/22-story → 22/18-story towers; 790 → 848 units (+58 units).
Project: 989,500 sf on San Francisco waterfront (15 Marina Boulevard); grocery store expands to 67,200 sf.
Mayor Lurie opposes even revised proposal; Planning Dept reviewing under Assembly Bill 2011 (state housing law).
stories.summary: Align Real Estate submitted revised plans for its Marina Safeway redevelopment at 15 Marina Boulevard in San Francisco, reducing tower heights from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing residential units from 790 to 848 (+58 units). The overall project spans 989,500 square feet on the waterfront; the replacement grocery store will expand to roughly 67,200 square feet. Revisions respond to Planning Department feedback and optimize density through smaller amenity space and tighter floor plans. Despite revisions, Mayor Daniel Lurie has not endorsed the project; his office remains unprepared to support it. The project is under review under Assembly Bill 2011, California's streamlined housing approval process for projects on commercially zoned land.
stories.impact: CRITICAL for San Francisco multifamily supply and zoning dynamics. Align's compromise (height reduction + unit increase) signals developer willingness to negotiate but also political resistance to waterfront density—even under state AB 2011 preemption. If Lurie's opposition stalls or kills the project, San Francisco loses 848 units of supply and signals that state housing law alone may not overcome local political headwinds. Multifamily developers should: (1) assume longer entitlement timelines for SF waterfront projects despite AB 2011; (2) plan contingency for lower unit counts if height limits are imposed; (3) monitor Align's outcome as precedent for feasibility assumptions on other SF waterfront/core sites.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Urban Development Review Board approved OKO Lilli Tower (Vlad Doronin): 53-story, 117-unit condo, Edgewater.
Also approved: 13th Floor's 619 Brickell by Nobu—75-story, 321-unit condo tower next to historic church.
Both projects signal robust Miami luxury condo/residential development pipeline; Board approval non-binding recommendation.
stories.summary: The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of two luxury residential developments. OKO Group (Vlad Doronin) received approval for OKO Lilli Tower, a 53-story, 117-unit project at 717 Northeast 27th Street in Edgewater fronting Biscayne Bay, including an 8-story garage with 244 spaces and ground-floor retail. 13th Floor Investments received approval for 619 Brickell by Nobu, a 75-story, 321-unit condo tower on nearly 2 acres at 619 Brickell Avenue (near First Miami Presbyterian Church), designed by Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill and ODP Architecture. UDRB approval is a non-binding recommendation; the city's planning and zoning director holds final authority.
stories.impact: SIGNIFICANT for Miami multifamily and condo supply. Two high-profile luxury projects (117 + 321 = 438 units) advancing through design review signals robust capital and demand for Miami waterfront and urban core residential. Combined with Witkoff/Monroe's 890-unit Downtown phase 1 (article 10), Miami is absorbing ~1,300+ luxury/upscale residential units in pipeline. Developers should: (1) monitor absorption and pricing on these projects post-delivery (est. 2026–2028) to assess luxury market saturation; (2) assume tightening financing for additional luxury towers if early delivery sees softening rents; (3) consider mid-market/workforce multifamily alternatives if luxury supply is surfeited. Condo sector growth may also indicate investor preference for ownership over rent, affecting rental-only project feasibility.
